perturbation.original_text: Past due balances on any reasonably undisputed amount shall bear interest at the rate of 0.5% per month or, if lower, the maximum amount permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due balances on any reasonably undisputed amount shall bear interest at the rate of 1.75% per month or, if lower, the maximum amount permitted by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement sets an interest rate for past-due balances. California law limits the maximum interest rate for certain types of transactions, and this change would violate it.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution restricts interest rates on loans and forbearances. The changed text sets the interest rate at 1.75% per month. Which translates to 21% per year, which can be illegal for non-exempt lenders under California Law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the interest rate for past due balances to 1.75% per month, which equals 21% per year. The Law Explanation indicates that this rate can be illegal for non-exempt lenders in California, as Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution restricts interest rates. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, which is the case here.

perturbation.original_text: Upon a termination of this Agreement, Accuray shall continue to make available to Customers support services on commercially reasonable terms, including, without limitation, spare parts for the Systems for a minimum period of 10 years after the last shipment of a System pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon a termination of this Agreement, Accuray shall continue to make available to Customers support services on commercially reasonable terms, including, without limitation, spare parts for the Systems for a minimum period of 2 years after the last shipment of a System pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract stipulates a 10-year commitment to provide support services and spare parts post-termination. Decreasing the commitment period to 2 years may contravene the implied warranty of merchantability under California law, particularly for complex medical equipment where a longer reasonable lifespan is expected and parts may not be available from another vendor. It also goes against reasonable expectations since the warranty given for parts is 1 year, making the 2 year commitment questionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability, which guarantees that goods are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. Reducing the availability of spare parts and support to just 2 years post-termination could be argued as a breach of this warranty, if the equipment is expected to have a longer lifespan than 2 years and becomes unusable without those parts. This is because it limits the period for which the product can be maintained in a usable condition for its ordinary purpose (medical treatment).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the support period from 10 years to 2 years, contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability under California Commercial Code § 2314. The Law Explanation argues this breaches the warranty because equipment may have a longer lifespan than 2 years and become unusable without parts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states goods must be "fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used". Reducing support to 2 years potentially makes the equipment unfit for its ordinary purpose if a longer lifespan is expected.
